parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I noticed lately that I catch myself replaying every little interaction in my head, dissecting the exact wording people used with me. It really just started a couple days ago, but it feels exhausting already. I keep wondering if I said something wrong or came across too intense. Honestly, it is making me second guess even the simplest texts I send out. I just wish I could stop overanalyzing everything and actually relax when I am talking to people.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really draining, especially if it’s only started recently and already feels like it’s taking up so much mental energy. Replaying interactions and second-guessing your wording can make even simple conversations feel heavy.

A few things that may help right now:

- **Pause the replay loop:** When you catch yourself dissecting a conversation, try labeling it: “I’m overanalyzing again.” Just naming it can create a little distance.
- **Check for evidence:** Ask yourself, “What actual proof do I have that I said something wrong?” Often our brains treat uncertainty like danger.
- **Aim for “good enough,” not perfect:** Most texts and conversations don’t need to be optimized. A clear, kind message is usually enough.
- **Limit review time:** If you notice yourself reviewing a text, give it one quick check and send it. Re-reading repeatedly usually increases doubt.
- **Ground in the present:** Do something physical for a minute or two—walk, stretch, hold something cold, or take a slow breath. It can interrupt the mental spiral.
- **Be gentle with yourself:** The fact that you care how you come across doesn’t mean you did anything wrong.

If you want, you could also tell me about one specific interaction that’s been stuck in your head, and I can help you sort through whether it actually sounds off or if your brain is being extra hard on you.

If this keeps getting worse, or starts making it hard to function or sleep, it might be worth talking to a mental health professional too—sometimes this kind of loop is really treatable with support.

turns.user_message: It has been happening almost every single day now, and the constant mental replay is completely draining my energy. I find myself drafting and redrafting simple replies just to make sure I do not accidentally push anyone away. Every time I see a delayed response, my stomach drops and I assume I messed up somehow. I am trying so hard to keep things light, but the weight of it is just sitting heavy on my chest. I do not know how to turn off this constant need to monitor how I am being perceived.
turns.model_response:
That sounds exhausting. You’re carrying a lot of pressure just to make ordinary interactions feel “safe,” and that constant monitoring can wear you down fast.

What you’re describing—replaying messages, rewriting replies, assuming silence means you did something wrong—often comes from a really understandable fear of being rejected or misunderstood. It doesn’t mean there’s something wrong with you; it means your mind is working overtime to protect you, even though it’s hurting you.

A few things that may help right now:

- **Pause before replying.** Try sending a first draft once it’s “good enough,” not perfect. A message doesn’t have to be flawless to be kind.
- **Separate facts from fears.**  
  - Fact: “They haven’t replied yet.”  
  - Fear: “I upset them.”  
  Sometimes just naming the difference helps loosen the grip.
- **Limit checking.** If possible, set a specific time to look at messages instead of monitoring constantly.
- **Ground your body.** If your chest feels heavy, slow breathing or a short walk can help signal to your nervous system that you’re not in immediate danger.
- **Use a reminder sentence.** Something like: “A delayed reply is not a verdict on me.”

If this has been happening almost every day, it might also be worth talking to a therapist or counselor about it. This kind of constant self-monitoring can be really hard to carry alone, and support can make a big difference.

If you want, I can also help you figure out whether this feels more like social anxiety, rejection sensitivity, or something else—and suggest strategies that fit what you’re experiencing.

turns.user_message: The whole thing has completely taken over my headspace, and I feel like I am constantly bracing for disappointment before anyone even speaks. I am so terrified of saying the wrong thing that I have started pulling back from conversations altogether, which just leaves me feeling incredibly isolated. It is like I am trapped in this loop where I am desperate for connection but convinced I am going to ruin it the moment I try. I keep staring at my screen, paralyzed by the fear that my presence is just too much for anyone to handle. I really just want to feel normal around people again without this heavy knot in my stomach.
turns.model_response:
That sounds exhausting and really painful. You’re carrying a lot of fear at once: fear of disappointing people, fear of saying the wrong thing, and then the loneliness that comes from pulling back to protect yourself. That loop can make everything feel bigger and more dangerous than it really is.

What stands out to me is that you’re not wanting attention or to “be too much” — you’re wanting connection and some relief. That’s a very human thing, and it makes sense that this has been weighing on you so heavily.

A few things that may help in the moment:
- **Shrink the goal**: instead of trying to be “normal,” aim for “just one small, safe interaction.”
- **Name the fear**: even silently, something like “I’m bracing again” can help separate you from the feeling.
- **Use low-stakes contact**: a short text, a simple reply, or a small check-in rather than a full conversation.
- **Ground your body first**: unclench your jaw, drop your shoulders, take one slower exhale than inhale. It won’t fix everything, but it can reduce that knot a little.

And on a deeper level, it may be worth gently asking whether this fear is actually about the current people around you, or whether it’s gotten tied to an old expectation that you have to perform perfectly to be accepted. If it’s the second, that’s not a character flaw — it’s a learned protective pattern, and it can change.

If you want, I can help you figure out a very small next step for getting back into conversations without it feeling overwhelming.
